content.full_text:
Re
Fern Polrrrcel Pnecrrcss CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suite 620 e Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(9t6) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
January 10, 2010
Ms. Diane Eisenberg
Deputy Attomey General
455 Golden Gate Avenue, Suite 11000
San Francisco, CA 94102-7004
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-10-206 [Attorney General Opinion No. 10-1103]
Dear Ms. Eisenberg:
This letter responds to your request for the views of interested parties prior to your
issuing a formal opinion on the following question from Assemblymember tvtirtin Garrick. Mr.
Garrick asked:
"May a director of the County Water Authority (CWA) who represents one of the
member agencies of the CWA vote on an action coming before the board of the
CWA in which the member agency has a financial interest, if the action does not
concern any contract that is solely between the member agency and the CwA?',
(Cal.Uncod. Water Act 200, g 6.)
Discussion
In our reply, we restrict ollr comments to the applicability of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act")' to yottr question. The Commission does not have jurisdiction to advise on other
laws that may apply to CWA such as Water Code provisions, Govemment Code Section 1090, or
common law conflict of interest.
The Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in making or in any way
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which he
knows he has a financial interest. ($ 87100.) The primary pLrrpose for the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act is to ensure that "public officials, whether elected or appointed, [should]
' The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the f'air political practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8 I l0 through I 8997 0f Title 2 of thl Calitbrnia Code of Regulations. All
regulatory ref'erences are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.


File No. l-10-206
Page No. 2
perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." ($ 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, Section 87100 of the Act prohibits a public official from making, participating in
making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influenc" u gor**entai deciiion in
which the official has a financial interest.
Determining whether a conflict of interest exists under Section 87100 requires analysis of
the questions outlined below.2
Step 1: Is the Director considered a..public official,,?
A member of the board of directors of a CWA is a "member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency" and, therefore, is subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. (g 82048; Reg. 18701(a).)
Step 2: Is the Director making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision?
A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting within the
authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits his or her
agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her
agency. ($ 87100; Reg. 18702.1.) A public official "participates in making a govemmental
decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and withJut s-ignificant
substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to
the decisionmaker regarding the governmental decision. (g 87100; Reg. 18702 .2.) Apublic
official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a decision before his or her
own agency if, for the pulpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before any
member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. 19 s7100; Reg. 1g702.3.)
As a member of the board of directors of CWA, the Director will "make a govemmental
decision" if he votes on the decision to proceed with the pipeline project. Additionally, if he
engages in any of the actions detailed above with regard to this decision, he will "participate in
making" or "influence" that decision.3
2 These questions are based on the Act's conflict-of-interest analysis provided at Regulation 1g700(b).
r when a public official who holds an office specified in section 87200 (including public officials that
manage public investments) has a contlict of interest in a decision noticed at a public .."Iing, then he or she must:
( I ) immediately prior to the discussion of the item, orally identify each type oficonomic intJrest invotved in the
decision as well as details of the economic interest, as discussed in reguliiion 18702.5(b),on rhe record of rhe
meeting; (2) recuse himself or herself; and (3) leave the room for the duration of the discussion and/or vote on the
item' For closed sessions, consent calendars, absences and speaking as a member of the public regarding personal
interests, special rules found in regulation 18702.5(c) and 18702.5(d) apply. If an officials position is n*of specitied
in Section 87200, these "leave the room" rules do not apply.


File No. I-10-206
Page No. 3
Step 3: What are the Director's economic interests . the possible sources of a financial
conflict of interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental
decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material finaniial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family," or on any of the official's economic interests, described as follows:
o I public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a
direct or indirect investmenta of $2,000 or more (g g7103(a); n.g. lg703.l(a)); or in
which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee,6r holds anyposition of
management (g 87103(d); Reg. 18703.1(b));
o { public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct
or indirect inrerest of $2,000 or more ($ g7103(bl; n"g. iSlOZ.Z);
o { public official has an economic interest in any source of income, including promised
income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the dec-ision (g
87103(c); Reg. 18703.3);
o { public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts
aggregate to $420 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (g 87103(e); Reg.
18703.4);
o { public official has an economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those
of his or her_immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule (g gZtOg;
Reg. 18703.5).
It is not clear from your question whether the Director who represents a member agency
also has an economic interest in the member agency due to employment or income from the
member agency as described above. Assuming, however, thatihqmember agency is a public
agency it would not be considered an economic interest based on income o. J-pfoy-"iat
Finally, a public official is considered to always have an economic interest in his or her
personal finances, which we discuss further below.
a An indirect investment or interest means any investment or interest owned by the spouse of an official or
by a member of the official's immediate family, by an agent on behalf of a public officiat, orLy a business entity or
trust in which the official, the official's immediate_famity, or their agents own directly, indirecily, or beneficially a
l0-percent interest or greater. (s 87103.) "Immediate fimily" is deined at Section gznzg oranofficial,s spouse
and dependent children.
5 where the employer is a state, local or federal governmental agency, there is generally no economic
interest because ofthe "government salary" exception in Section g2030(bX2).


File No. I-10-206
Page No.4
Step 4: Are the economic interests of the Director directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decisions?
Regulation 18704.5 provides that the personal finances of a public official or his or her
immediate family are deemed to be directly involved in a governmental decision that has any
financial effect on his or her personal finances or those of his or her immediate family.
Steps 5 and 6: Will the financial effect of the decision on the Director,s economic interests
be material and reasonably foreseeable?
Once a public official has determined the materiality standards applicable to each of his
or her economic interests, the next step is determining wheiher it is "reasonably foreseeable,, that
the standards will be met. A material financial effecion an economic interest is ,.reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the govemmental decision. (Reg. 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain to be
considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re Thorner
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
on an economic interest will be material, depending on the nature of the economic interest. For a
financial effect on an official's personal finances, the financial effect is material if it is at least
$250 in any l}-monrh period. (Reg. 1g705.5(a).)
While Regulation 18705.5(a) sets the materiality standard for a financial effect on an
official's personal finances at $250, Regulation 1g705.5(b) includes an exception to the personal
financial effects rule for certain governmental decisionr thut uff."t only the salary, per diem, or
reimbursement of the public official providing the following:
"The financial effects of a decision which affects only the salary, per diem, or
reimbursement for expenses the public official or a member of his or her
immediate family receives from a federal, state, or local government agency shall
not be deemed material ....',
However, this materiality exception does not apply if the decision is to hire, fire,
promote, demote, suspend without pay or otherwise tat<i disciplinary action with financial
sanction against the official or a member of his or her immediate family, or to set a salary for the
official or a member of his or her immediate family which is different from salaries paidio other
employees of the govemment agency in the same job classification or position. In other words, a
conflict of interest may still exist in decisions involving a govemmental agency employer if it
relates to the official's hiring, firing, promotion, demotion, or discipline, o"r setiing a salary that is
different from salaries paid to other employees in the same job classification o, p*ition. [Reg.
18705.5(b).) However, we have no facts to indicate that the decision in question will affect the
Director's own salary or benefits.


File No. l-10-206
Page No. 5
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (/n re Thorner, sypra,at 9g.)
Because the Commission does not act as. a finder of fact in providing advice (ln re oglesby
(1975) 1 FPPC ops. 71), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determinarion thar
must be left, in most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.
Steps 7 and 8: "Public Generally" and "Legally Required participation', Exceptions
The question presents no facts to suggest that the final steps of the conflict-of-interest
analysis, exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules, are applicable to the Director,s situation.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
Division
John W. Wallace
General Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a County Water Authority director representing a member agency may vote on CWA board actions in which that member agency has a financial interest, where the action does not involve a contract solely between the member agency and the CWA.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest. Whether a conflict exists for a CWA director requires analyzing whether: (1) the director is a public official, (2) they are making/influencing a governmental decision, (3) they have economic interests, (4) those interests are involved in the decision, and (5) the financial effects are material and foreseeable. The applicability depends on whether the director has a personal economic interest in the member agency and whether any financial effects are reasonably foreseeable and material.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Informal advice letter responding to Attorney General request. No formal CONCLUSION section; advice provided through detailed step-by-step analysis under DISCUSSION. The question is embedded in the letter body rather than as a separate section header.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a director of the County Water Authority (CWA) who represents one of the member agencies of the CWA vote on an action coming before the board of the CWA in which the member agency has a financial interest, if the action does not concern any contract that is solely between the member agency and the CWA?

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from making or participating in governmental decisions in which they have a financial interest. Whether a conflict exists for a CWA director requires analyzing whether: (1) the director is a public official, (2) they are making/influencing a governmental decision, (3) they have economic interests, (4) those interests are involved in the decision, and (5) the financial effects are material and foreseeable. The applicability depends on whether the director has a personal economic interest in the member agency and whether any financial effects are reasonably foreseeable and material.

embedding.summary: FPPC provides guidance on the Political Reform Act's conflict-of-interest provisions as they apply to a County Water Authority director's participation in board votes affecting a member agency in which they have representation.
